# Testing Strategies in a Microservice Architecture

> Source: Toby Clemson (ThoughtWorks), published on martinfowler.com — 18 November 2014
> Original: 25-slide Infodeck. This document is a faithful transcription of all slides and diagrams.

---

## 1. Introduction

There has been a shift in service based architectures over the last few years towards smaller, more focused "micro" services. There are many benefits to this approach such as the ability to independently deploy, scale and maintain each component and parallelize development across multiple teams.

However, as with most architectural decisions, there are trade-offs to be made. The testing strategies that applied for monolithic in-process applications need to be reconsidered.

Testing in this context is discussed to address:
- The additional testing complexity of multiple independently deployable components.
- How to have tests and the application monitor how multiple teams each act as guardians for different services.

---

## 2. What are Microservices & Their Anatomy

### 2.1 Internal Module Structure

Often, microservices display similar internal structure consisting of some or all of the following layers. Any testing strategy employed should aim to provide coverage to **each layer** and **between layers** whilst remaining lightweight.

> Gateways and HTTP Client are **vertical side bars** spanning the height of Service Layer + Domain + Repositories — they are parallel to the domain stack, not children of it.

![Slide 4: Module Structure](data:image/png;base64,)

**Module Descriptions:**

| Module | Colour | Layer | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Resources | Green (Protocol) | Top | Maps HTTP requests ↔ domain objects; thin sanity-check layer; returns protocol-specific response |
| Service Layer | Teal (Domain) | | Coordinates domain objects to fulfil a business transaction |
| Domain | Teal (Domain) | Centre | Core business logic; the richest part of the service |
| Gateways | Purple (External) | Side bar | Encapsulates messaging to external services/APIs |
| HTTP Client | Purple (External) | Side bar | Makes outbound HTTP calls to other microservices |
| Repositories | Teal (Domain) | | Abstracts persistence operations on domain objects |
| Data Mappers / ORM | Pink (Persistence) | Bottom | Maps domain objects ↔ database representation |

### 2.2 Services Connect Over Networks and Use External Datastores

Microservices handle requests by passing messages between each of the relevant modules to form a response. The presence of network partitions affects the style of testing employed — tests of integration modules may fail for reasons outside of the team's control.

![Slide 5: Connections](data:image/png;base64,)

> **Legend addition in this slide:** Logical Boundary (inner dashed line separating pure domain from integration code) and Connection (arrows between modules).

### 2.3 Multiple Services Work Together as a System

In larger systems, multiple teams each have responsibility for different **bounded contexts**. Testing concerns for **external services** differ from internal ones — fewer guarantees can be made about their interface and availability.

![Slide 6: Multi-service System](data:image/png;base64,)

---

## 3. Unit Testing

### 3.1 Two Styles

Services are commonly a **rich domain** surrounded by **plumbing and coordination code**. Both styles of unit testing play an important role.

![Slide 8: Unit Testing Styles](data:image/png;base64,)

| Style | Marker | Applied to | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sociable** | Solid red square | Domain | Logic is highly state-based; real collaborators add value |
| **Solitary** | Dashed pink square | Resources, Service Layer, Repositories | Coordination/plumbing code; Test Doubles keep tests fast |
| *(none)* | — | Gateways, HTTP Client, Data Mappers/ORM | Covered by Integration tests instead |

**Why Sociable for Domain:**
Domain logic often manifests as complex calculations and a collection of state transitions. Since these types of logic are highly state-based, there is little value in trying to isolate the units. Real domain objects should be used for all collaborators of the unit under test.

**Why Solitary for coordination code:**
Resources, Service Layer, and Repositories are coordination/plumbing code — their responsibility is delegation and mapping. Using Test Doubles makes them fast and focused.

### 3.2 Limits of Unit Testing

Unit testing alone doesn't provide guarantees about the behaviour of the system. Modules are tested in isolation — but there is no coverage of:
- How modules work **together** to form a complete service.
- Interactions with **remote dependencies** (External Datastore, other services).

![Slide 9: Unit Testing Limits](data:image/png;base64,)

> The diagram shows Unit Tested markers (red squares) on Resources, Service Layer, Domain, and Repositories only. Gateways, HTTP Client, Data Mappers/ORM, and External Datastore remain uncovered.

To verify that each module correctly interacts with its collaborators, **more coarse-grained testing is required**.

---

## 4. Integration Testing

> **"An integration test verifies the communication paths and interactions between components to detect interface defects."**

### 4.1 Definition

Integration tests collect modules together and test them as a **subsystem** to verify they collaborate as intended. They exercise communication paths to check for incorrect assumptions each module has about how to interact with its peers.

In microservice architectures, integration tests are typically used to verify interactions between **layers of integration code** (Repositories, Gateways, HTTP Client) and the **external components** to which they integrate (data stores, caches, other microservices).

### 4.2 Integration Test Boundary

![Slide 11: Integration Test Boundary](data:image/png;base64,)

> The yellow dashed **Integration Test Boundary** wraps the Data Mappers/ORM layer **and** its connection to the External Datastore — i.e., it covers both sides of the integration point. A separate boundary exists around Gateways/HTTP Client and their external connections.

**Key advice:**
- Tests in this style provide fast feedback when refactoring integration modules.
- However they have **more than one reason to fail** — if the external component becomes unavailable or breaks its contract.
- Write only a **handful** of integration tests to cover the communication boundary.
- Consider having failing builds when external outages occur so they don't block development.

---

## 5. Component Testing

> **"A component test limits the scope of the exercised software to a portion of the system under test, manipulating the system through internal code interfaces and using test doubles to isolate the code under test from other components."**

Without component tests, we have no confidence that the microservice **as a whole** meets business requirements. While end-to-end tests can achieve this, more isolation gives faster and more repeatable feedback.

### 5.1 In-Process Component Tests

Tests communicate with the microservice through an **internal interface** — e.g., Spring's `MockMvc` (JVM) or `plasma` (.NET). External dependencies are replaced with **in-memory stubs inside the same process**.

![Slide 14: In-Process Component Test](data:image/png;base64,)

> The orange/yellow dashed **Component Test Boundary** encloses the entire service. External service stubs are in-memory and live **within** the boundary. The test harness communicates via internal shim (not real HTTP).

**Trade-off:** Maximum speed and simplicity. No real network calls. Slightly less fidelity than a real deployment.

### 5.2 Internal Resources

Exposing internal controls on a **separate endpoint** brings additional testing simplicity (monitoring, maintenance, debugging):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph API["Service API"]
        Pub["Public Resources /api/"]
        Int["Internal Resources /internal/"]
    end

    Client["External Client"] --> Pub
    Ops["Ops / Test Harness"] --> Int

    Int --> FF["Feature Flags"]
    Int --> HC["Health Check"]
    Int --> Metrics["Metrics / Timings"]
    Int --> DBCmd["DB Commands"]
```

These endpoints can require their own authentication or be locked down at the network level.

### 5.3 Out-of-Process Component Tests

Exercise the **fully deployed artifact** over real HTTP. Stubbing complexity is pushed into the test harness — external services are replaced by **stub servers** (e.g., mountebank, WireMock).

![Slide 16: Out-of-Process Component Test](data:image/png;base64,)

> The red dashed **Component Test Boundary** encloses the deployed service plus its external stubs. The stubs live outside the service process but inside the test boundary. The External Datastore is a real instance (e.g., Testcontainers).

**Trade-off:** Higher fidelity (real network, real artifact). Slower and more complex to set up.

**Example stub tools:** mountebank, WireMock, moco — support dynamic programming, fixture-based stubs, and record-replay.

---

## 6. Contract Testing

> **"An integration contract test is a test at the boundary of an external service verifying that it meets the contract expected by a consuming service."**

### 6.1 Why Contract Tests Are Needed

By combining unit, integration and component testing, we achieve high coverage *within* each service. However, we still have no tests that ensure external dependencies meet the microservice's expectations and that services collaborate correctly to provide business-valuable features.

![Slide 17: Combination Leading to Contract Tests](data:image/png;base64,)

> This slide shows a multi-service diagram with Unit, Integration, and Component test boundaries all applied — and highlights the remaining gap at **service-to-service boundaries** that contract tests address.

### 6.2 Consumer-Driven Contracts

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Producer["Producer: id, name, age"]

    Producer -- "same full response" --> CA["Contract A: couples to id, name"]
    Producer -- "same full response" --> CB["Contract B: couples to id, name, age"]
    Producer -- "same full response" --> CC["Contract C: couples to id, age"]

    CA --> ConsA["Consumer A"]
    CB --> ConsB["Consumer B"]
    CC --> ConsC["Consumer C"]

    style Producer fill:#CE93D8,stroke:#9C27B0
    style CA fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style CB fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style CC fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style ConsA fill:#80CBC4,stroke:#00897B
    style ConsB fill:#80CBC4,stroke:#00897B
    style ConsC fill:#80CBC4,stroke:#00897B
```

![Slide 19: Consumer Contracts Define Service Contract](data:image/png;base64,)

> **Important:** All three consumers receive the **same full JSON response** `{"id": 5, "name": "James", "age": 24}`. What differs is the **subset each consumer couples to** — Contract A/B/C represent different field dependencies. The sum of all consumer contracts defines the overall service contract the Producer must honour.

**How it works:**
1. Each **consumer** writes tests expressing exactly which fields/schema it depends on from the producer.
2. These consumer contract test suites are **packaged and run in the producer's build pipeline**.
3. If the producer makes a change that breaks any consumer's contract, the build fails immediately.
4. These are **NOT end-to-end tests** — they only verify that the input/output schema at the boundary contains what the consumer requires.

---

## 7. End-to-End Testing

> **"An end-to-end test verifies that a system meets external requirements and achieves its goals, testing the entire system, from end to end."**

### 7.1 E2E Test Boundary

The system is treated as a **black box**, driven through public interfaces (GUI, REST API). External services outside the team's control are typically **stubbed at the system boundary**.

![Slide 21: E2E Test Boundary](data:image/png;base64,)

> The large pink dashed **E2E Test Boundary** encloses only the services owned by the team. External services managed by other teams are stubbed outside the boundary. JSON `{"..."}` labels on connections show the inter-service communication.

**Why E2E tests are valuable in microservices:**
- Adds coverage of the gaps that other test types cannot reach (message routing, system configuration).
- Ensures business value remains intact after large architectural changes (service splits/merges).

### 7.2 Guidelines for Managing E2E Complexity

E2E tests involve many more moving parts → more risk, flakiness, high maintenance burden (async system, async backend processes between services).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1["1. Keep the suite small"]
    G2["2. Focus on user journeys"]
    G3["3. Test data independence"]
    G4["4. Infrastructure as code"]

    G1 --> G2 --> G3 --> G4
```

**On guideline ④ — infrastructure-as-code:**
Snowflake environments are a source of non-determinism. By building a **fresh environment for every E2E suite execution**, reliability improves and the deployment logic itself is validated.

---

## 8. Summary & Testing Pyramid

### 8.1 Microservices Provide More Testing Options

By breaking a system into small, well-defined services, boundaries are exposed that were previously hidden. These boundaries provide flexibility in the type and level of testing required:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Core Business Service - high investment"]
        T1["Unit + Integration + Component + Contract + E2E"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Supporting Service - medium investment"]
        T2["Unit + Integration + Component"]
    end
    subgraph S3["Experimental Service - low investment"]
        T3["Unit only"]
    end

    style S1 fill:#EF9A9A,stroke:#C62828
    style S2 fill:#FFE082,stroke:#F57F17
    style S3 fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#2E7D32
```

### 8.2 Testing Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E2E["End-to-End: few, slow, full system"]
    CT["Contract: per service boundary, consumer-driven"]
    COMP["Component: per service, isolated with stubs"]
    IT["Integration: per adapter, external boundary only"]
    UT["Unit: many, fast, domain + coordination"]

    UT --> IT --> COMP --> CT --> E2E
```

### 8.3 Summary — All Test Types on One Diagram

![Slide 24: In Summary](data:image/png;base64,)

> The summary diagram overlays all five test types on a single multi-service view:
> - **Red squares** = Unit Tested modules
> - **Yellow dashed** = Integration Test Boundary (tight around each adapter + external component)
> - **Orange/salmon dashed** = Component Test Boundary (whole service)
> - **Yellow striped** = Contract Test Boundary (at service-to-service edges)
> - *E2E boundary would enclose the entire system*

### 8.4 Complete Reference Table

| Test Type | Scope | What it verifies | JVM Tools | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit (Sociable)** | Class + real domain collaborators | Domain logic correctness | JUnit 5 | ⚡⚡⚡⚡⚡ |
| **Unit (Solitary)** | Class + test doubles | Coordination / mapping logic | JUnit 5 + Mockito | ⚡⚡⚡⚡⚡ |
| **Integration** | Module + real external adapter | Communication path to DB / cache / broker | @DataJpaTest + Testcontainers | ⚡⚡⚡ |
| **Component (in-process)** | Whole service, in-memory stubs | Business requirements met in isolation | SpringBootTest + MockMvc | ⚡⚡⚡⚡ |
| **Component (out-of-process)** | Deployed artifact + stub servers | Service runs correctly as real artifact | Testcontainers + WireMock | ⚡⚡ |
| **Contract** | Service API boundary | Consumer expectations met by producer | Pact / Spring Cloud Contract | ⚡⚡⚡⚡ |
| **End-to-End** | Entire system | Business goals achieved end-to-end | REST-assured + Docker Compose | ⚡ |

### 8.5 Layer × Test Type Coverage Matrix

> Corrected from original slides: Gateways, HTTP Client, and Data Mappers/ORM have **no Unit test markers** — they are covered by Integration tests.

| Layer | Unit Solitary | Unit Sociable | Integration | Component | Contract | E2E |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Resources | ✓ | | | ✓ | | ✓ |
| Service Layer | ✓ | | | ✓ | | ✓ |
| Domain | | ✓ | | ✓ | | ✓ |
| Gateways | | | ✓ | stub | ✓ | ✓ |
| HTTP Client | | | ✓ | stub | ✓ | ✓ |
| Repositories | ✓ | | ✓ | stub | — | ✓ |
| Data Mappers / ORM | | | ✓ | stub | — | ✓ |
